common_name: Jersey Devil Tomato (New Jersey Heirloom Banana Paste Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting after last frost; Jersey Devil is an indeterminate vine that benefits from sturdy caging or trellising. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer production. The elongated banana-shaped fruits (6–8 inches, pointed at the blossom end) are meaty with very few seeds — excellent for sauce, roasting, and drying, where the low moisture and dense flesh concentrate flavor and reduce cooking time.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; transplant to full sun in rich, well-drained soil after last frost. Stake or cage plants before or at transplanting. Jersey Devil is a New Jersey heirloom paste tomato with an immediately recognizable elongated, banana-shaped to horn-shaped fruit form: 5–8 inches long, tapered to a sharp point at the blossom end, with bright red skin and very dense, meaty flesh. The distinctive shape — sometimes described as resembling a long red chili pepper at a glance — makes Jersey Devil one of the most visually unusual paste tomatoes. The eating quality matches the visual appeal: the flesh is very dense and dry with minimal gel and seeds, a high solid content that makes it ideal for tomato sauce (concentrates quickly without extended cooking), sun-dried tomatoes, and oven-roasted preparations where the low moisture prevents the pan from becoming soupy. Jersey Devil is an indeterminate vine that produces heavily over a long season; the fruits ripen mid-season (78–88 days from transplant) and continue until frost. The variety is strongly connected to New Jersey, where it appears in the regional heirloom seed tradition alongside other East Coast paste types.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Jersey Devil tomato variety notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Paste tomato collection
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Heirloom Paste Tomato Variety Evaluation

common_name: Caspian Pink Tomato (Russian Heirloom Low-Acid Beefsteak Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Caspian Pink is an indeterminate beefsteak that produces large, heavy fruits (8–14 oz) and benefits from strong stake or sturdy cage support. In zone 9, transplant March–April for July–August harvest. Like many large beefsteak types, Caspian Pink requires warm nights to set fruit well — the Gulf South's warm summer nights are typically favorable for fruit set in this variety.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; transplant to full sun in fertile, deeply prepared soil. Stake securely — the large, heavy fruits can pull down unsupported vines. Caspian Pink is a Ukrainian-Russian heirloom beefsteak tomato introduced to the United States by Marianna Kezenberger, an immigrant from the Caspian Sea region of the former Soviet Union — the name references both the geographic origin and the distinctive color. The variety is a large-fruited, low-acid pink-skinned beefsteak type: fruits weigh 8–14 oz or more, with the flattened, slightly irregular, shouldered shape characteristic of traditional beefsteaks. The skin is thin, smooth, and a clear rose-pink rather than true red, indicating the absence of the yellow skin gene — a characteristic associated with lower perceived acidity and sweeter flavor in tomatoes. The flavor profile is rich, complex, and less sharp than red-skinned beefsteaks — noticeably sweeter at the same sugar content because lower acid allows sweetness to predominate. Caspian Pink is consistently rated among the best-tasting pink heirloom beefsteaks in comparative tastings, often mentioned in the same conversation as Brandywine and Cherokee Purple as a top-tier beefsteak for eating quality.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Caspian Pink tomato variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Russian heirloom tomato collection
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Heirloom tomato tasting trial notes

common_name: Honey Nut Squash (Cornell Mini Butternut Sweet Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 2–3 weeks earlier for zone 5–6 shorter seasons; in zone 9, plant March–April for fall harvest. Honey Nut requires the same long season as standard butternut (100–110 days) but stores well through winter (3–4 months in a cool, dry location) and cures similarly — allow 2 weeks at warm temperatures (80–85°F) to harden the skin after harvest. The mini size (5–6 oz) means individual fruits serve 1–2 people perfectly.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in hills of 3 seeds after last frost; thin to 2 plants per hill. Cure harvested fruits at 80–85°F for 2 weeks to harden skin, then store in cool, dry conditions. Honey Nut is a miniature butternut-type winter squash developed at Cornell University by plant breeder Michael Mazourek, introduced commercially around 2011–2015 — a squash that has rapidly gained status as one of the most innovative new vegetable introductions of the decade. The standard butternut squash (Waltham Butternut and similar) is widely considered one of the best-flavored winter squashes but weighs 3–5 lbs — more than most households can comfortably consume in one meal. Honey Nut produces single-serving-sized fruits of only 4–6 oz, with the same classic butternut shape (pear-shaped with a long cylindrical neck and round base) compressed to a palm-sized scale. Beyond the practical size advantage, Honey Nut has exceptional flavor that often outperforms standard butternut in blind tastings — the small size means the fruits must mature fully before harvest, when starches have converted to sugar, resulting in a consistently sweeter, more intense squash flavor. The deep orange flesh is very sweet, smooth-textured, and rich — ideal for roasting (a single halved fruit roasts in 30–35 minutes, faster than the 45–60 minutes for full-size butternut), soups, and purees.
sources: Cornell University Plant Breeding — Honey Nut squash development by Michael Mazourek
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Honey Nut squash introduction and notes
Bon Appétit magazine — Honey Nut squash feature (2015)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 2–3 weeks earlier; in zone 9, plant March–April for fall harvest. Sunshine ripens to a bright orange-red skin (unlike traditional green kabocha types), making ripe fruit easy to identify — harvest when skin has fully transitioned to the characteristic deep orange and stem begins to dry. Cure at warm temperatures (80–85°F) for 10 days after harvest; flavor and sweetness improve significantly with 30–60 days of storage after curing.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in hills of 3 seeds; thin to 2 plants per hill. Harvest when skin turns deep orange and stem begins to cork; cure 10 days at warm temperature before storage. Sunshine is a 2004 All-America Selections-winning kabocha-type (Japanese pumpkin) winter squash developed by Sakata Seed that distinguishes itself from all other kabocha types by its brilliant, uniform burnt-orange to brick-red skin — traditional kabocha is deep green with lighter stripes, making Sunshine visually unique. The bright color is not merely ornamental: Sunshine's orange pigmentation indicates high beta-carotene content (kabocha squash is already one of the most nutritious squash types, and Sunshine adds greater orange pigment depth). The flesh is deep orange, extremely dry and dense with a floury, almost chestnut-like texture — the characteristic kabocha texture that sets it apart from the moister, stringier flesh of acorn, butternut, or delicata types. The flavor is intensely sweet (some tasters compare it to sweet potato with richer, more complex notes) and becomes even sweeter after storage. Sunshine produces medium-sized (3–4 lb) flat, pumpkin-shaped fruits on compact vines. It is consistently rated among the top-flavored winter squashes in university trials and has become a fixture at farmers markets where the orange color and flat kabocha form immediately distinguish it from other squash types.
sources: All-America Selections — Sunshine squash AAS winner 2004
Sakata Seed — Sunshine kabocha variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Winter squash variety flavor comparison

common_name: Galia Melon (Israeli Hybrid Netted Green-Flesh Muskmelon)

planting.note: Direct sow in hills after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Galia requires warm conditions throughout its development and performs well in the Gulf South's warm summer climate. In zone 9, plant March–April for June–August harvest. Reduce irrigation as fruits approach maturity to concentrate sugars. Harvest when the skin transitions from green to golden-yellow and a sweet, tropical fragrance is detectable at the stem end — the skin color change from green to yellow-gold is the primary harvest indicator for Galia.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in hills of 3 seeds after last frost; thin to 2 plants per hill. Harvest when skin turns yellow-gold and a sweet tropical aroma is detectable at the stem end. Galia is an Israeli hybrid muskmelon type developed in the 1970s at the Newe Ya'ar Research Center by Dr. Zvi Karchi, named for his daughter — an F1 hybrid that combines the netted, aromatic skin characteristics of cantaloupe/muskmelon with the pale green, spicy-sweet flesh of honeydew, creating a novel flavor profile found in no other commonly available melon type. The round to slightly oval fruits (3–5 lb) have heavily netted, yellow-green to yellow-gold skin at maturity (the skin begins green and transitions to yellow-gold as the melon ripens — a useful visual harvest cue not present in cantaloupe types that stay tan). The flesh is pale green to cream-green, aromatic, and extremely sweet with a distinctive tropical character — floral, spicy, and complex, distinctly different from both orange-fleshed cantaloupe and the blander honeydew. Galia melons are widely grown and marketed throughout Europe, Israel, and Central America and have become increasingly available in American specialty markets, where they are often sold under the name 'Israeli melon.' The flavor is among the most complex and interesting of any melon type, making Galia a top choice for gardeners who want to explore beyond standard cantaloupe.
sources: Newe Ya'ar Research Center, Israel — Galia melon development documentation
Seed Savers Exchange — Galia melon variety notes
University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Melon Production Guide
